The main right-to-repair claims in the discourse cluster into a few recurring narratives:

- Political “promise” claim (farm equipment): Multiple viral posts assert that Donald Trump said “right to repair is coming to our farmers,” often paired with the allegation that John Deere has treated farmers unfairly “for decades.”
- Military right-to-repair claim: Posts argue that right-to-repair provisions for military equipment were removed from the defense authorization process (often described as being “stripped” from the NDAA/defense bill), despite supposed bipartisan/administration support, and that restrictions harm readiness.
- Auto right-to-repair policy claim: Posts promote a federal “REPAIR Act/Repair Act,” claiming it would guarantee access to independent repair shops and ease restrictions as vehicles become more software-dependent.
- Software lock-in / subscription claim (vehicles): A prominent YouTube narrative alleges Hyundai requires a computer connection plus subscription software to perform basic service (rear brake pads) on newer Ioniq 5 EVs—framed as an escalation in repair lockouts.
- Consumer/tech sustainability claims: Posts claim right-to-repair is linked to reducing waste and premature replacement, including a quantitative assertion that “70%+ of corporate laptops entering e-waste streams still have 4–6 years of usable life,” plus the claim that “Europe uses a repairability index.”

A key caveat from the evidence attached to these extracted claims: many are widely repeated but not well-supported by corroborating reporting within the retrieved sources in this run (several claims
